sourceDesc.p: Produced from a Project Gutenberg HTML source using gutenberg-text-splitter.

body.div:
BOOK XIII.
VOLTAIC ELECTRICITY.
CHAPTER VII. Magneto-electric Induction. FARADAY’S discovery that, in combinations like those in which a voltaic current was known to produce motion, motion would produce a voltaic current, naturally excited great attention among the scientific men of Europe. The general nature of his discovery was communicated by letter26 to M. Hachette at Paris, in December, 1831; and experiments having the like results were forthwith made by MM. Becquerel and Ampère at Paris, and MM. Nobili and Antinori at Florence.
